comment_content:
Overall I think the content is quite good to introduce you to database. After taking this course, you can expect that it is not too hard for you to design database tables (so that it's not confusing and scattered), write SQL queries, and make a simple application built with it. Quite practical in my opinion.

I somewhat integrated some of the concepts from this course to organize my COMP3111 project (yes, pls use proper .db file or at least .json, don't use cursed .txt databases. Integrity constraints will also help you a lot)~

I do agree that second half feels like something that you won't need to know if you are just using a database. But I think it is a good addition for the completeness of the course. For example, in file organization you can understand why indexes are actually important, in transactions you can learn how database actually handles concurrent users in a large application. As a plus, you also get some experience to work on assigment4 on integrating your knowledge to actual ODBC application.

However, the course does not really go into NoSQL queries under Prof Wilfred.

== Contents ==
- E/R Diagrams
- Database Schema
- Relational Algebra
- SQL Queries (Big Topic! Was also on the Finals)
- Functional Dependencies (In different offerings, the amount of this topic in midterm varies. Also a big and rather confusing topic the first time you encounter it)

[Midterm is arranged around here]

- Physical Database and File Organization
- Transactions (How Multiple Users can be handled)
- NoSQL and MapReduce
- Database Connectivity (ODBC, JDBC, etc. -- just theory)

=== Comments on Topics ===
The part regarding E/R diagram might be intimidating at first. The assignment description was also not that clear (however, I think writing assumptions + asking questions helps). However questions in exam are generally not ambiguous and clear. I also find E/R diagram to make more sense than *cough* UML diagram from COMP3111 (btw note that the cardinality constraint, when you use numbers, if flipped -- you might take COMP3111 at the same time if you are a COMP student)

btw In general if you find the assignment description confusing just ask. The teaching team and Prof is quite nice. (emailed Prof on a weekend to ask about what he meant on assignment 3 🫠🫠🫠, he replied :3)

Schema is an OK topic, just remember how to convert, understand primary key, foreign key, etc.

Relational Algebra and SQL is slightly challenging. I don't know proper SQL before the course. My suggestion is to practice SQL a lot, places to practice: https://www.sql-practice.com/ and before exam you may try questions from UWash CSE 344 Exams (I cannot find the link when writing this review, let me update later 🤔-- the questions here are far harder than 3311). Then, you just make sure you can remember RA notations and you're fine.

FD is uninituitive at first, but you can understand it from attending the tutorial and do the problem set slides on the course website. Finding extra practice problems online also helps.

Physical Database and File Organization is quite confusing (mostly because the questions in the tutorial is quite different). The more important topics are actually (according to past papers and the exam):
- Calculating blocking factor and how many pages needed to store it
- Calculating a single Nested Loop, Sort-Merge, Hash Join Costs
- B+ Trees dry run
See the past paper (released arround exam time) and learn why the answers are the way they are.

Transactions needs some practice, but just try to learn the rules and pay attention in the tutorial. Also locking protocol can be confusing, take some time to review it. If you are taking COMP3511 at this time, some intuition from OS topic on concurrency will help.

The rest are mostly theories (NoSQL and Connectivity, they will appear mostly in MCQs except MapReduce).

Some exam questions solutions are indeed misleading, you can ask Prof about it (and Prof also admits that there are mistakes).
comment_teaching:
[Kind of writing this review because I forgot to fill SFQ 😅, anyway... if you got credits for elective and see COMP3311 is under Prof Wilfred. I encourage you to take it~]

comment_workload:
The workload is mostly from understanding the lecture materials and practicing 😅😅😅.

The assignments are actually not taking that much of your time (1-2 hours... but please do spend time checking them because they take a large percentage of your final grade (like please bother yourself opening Oracle SQL Developer (and making testcases) for assignment 3 and 4, it is worth the hassle). Please also do it early so you can ask Prof if there are any ambiguities in questions phrasing.

The 4 assignments is not as intimidating as you'd expect. You can expect each assignment to be something like COMP3511 assignments difficulty (and seriously why COMP3511 assignments take up so much grade percentage?), but slightly easier.

Due to questions ambiguity I did get some mark off on assignment 1 and 2, but if you are in this situation, I think you do not need to force yourself to haggle over it, the mark deduction is small for my case. I do hope that assignments can be more clear in the future, though.
comment_grading:
== Grading Scheme ==
> 10% Attendance
- 5% Tutorial Attendance (10 tutorials, 0.5% each)
- 4% Lab Attendance (8 labs, 0.5% each)
- 1% Submit all assignments on time

Note: There are no assignments for the labs/tutorial. Attendance are recorded by TAs. Paying full attention is not neccessary, but if you do it reduces workload for your review. Also some lab SQLs can be tested (e.g. trigger and PL/SQL appeared in finals💀 -- I need to speedrun write my cheatsheet). I do understand that  compulsory attendance is annoying but I guess this one is ok...

> 30% Assignments
- 6% Assignment 1 (E/R Diagram)
- 6% Assignment 2 (Schema)
- 9% Assignment 3 (SQL)
- 9% Assignment 4 (ODBC)

> Midterm 30% or 0% (can transfer to final)

> Final 30% or 60% (if transferred to final)

Your grade will be the maximum of the two schemes.

The topics in midterm are generally not tested again in finals, except for SQL.

Also actually some MCQ questions are taken from his sample, with slight modifications (I don't really like this.. but I guess there are benefits for doing sample papers I guess... I might be screwed if this is not the case) 😅

== Statistics ==
My grade is in (...) for your reference

Anyway.. if you did well in the midterm you have a big advantage~ But if you don't, you can have a comeback in the finals

comment_content:
After this class is over, you will still be wondering what a "Database Management System" is.

Content up to the midterm covers:
- E-R Diagrams
- Relational Database Design
- Relational Algebra
- SQL
This content is fine, although you should probably learn some SQL before taking this course, because the midterm scores are competitive.

Post-midterm content up to the final covers:
- MongoDB
- Indexing (B+ tree)
- Query Processing
- Transaction Management

Side note, MongoDB is straight up the worst language I have ever used, the syntax is so unbelievably annoying. It does have some similarity with SQL, but they will not explicitly explain that to you.

At this point, every lecture is just straight up useless since they do not actually teach the DBMS architecture properly. I would STRONGLY recommend just watching Berkeley's CS186 course on YouTube.

https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLYp4IGUhNFmw8USiYMJvCUjZe79fvyYge

Like, I went to every lecture and understood nothing. Then, before the final, I watched the Berkeley CS186 videos and finally learned what the fuck an "index nested loop join" was, among various other things.

The content of this class (DBMS) is very useful and important in industry, but the way they teach it is just astonishingly abhorrent, to say the least. If you are able, please take this class at a different university to save your sanity.
comment_teaching:
The professor is a nice, old man, but every lecture he just reads off the slide deck. Nobody pays attention, because you will fall asleep if you try to. Don't even try to understand the lectures on the harder concepts in the second half of the class, you will just be more confused. The PowerPoint slides are a clusterfuck of incomprehensible gibberish.

Tutorial and Labs are fine, pray your TA can explain the concepts better than the professor.

Do not try to challenge or appeal your scores, they will deduct more marks instead.
comment_workload:
There is a worksheet for every lecture, tutorial, and lab, each due at 11:00pm the following day. Annoying, but manageable.

Three major assignments:

2. SQL Database
3. SQL + MongoDB queries

Not too hard, you can complete in a reasonable amount of time. However, do not expect to get full marks, they can be quite harsh with the grading.

Spend most of your time studying/preparing for the exams.

comment_content:
Don’t be fooled by the occasional positive reviews of this course—I can’t imagine how it received any praise at all. If you dare to enrol, your experience will be nothing short of “eye-opening” and “unprecedented”—but not in a good way. I’ve asked plenty of my classmates, and NOT A SINGLE ONE had anything positive to say. Let me be clear: I’m not targeting those who left good reviews, but please allow me to say this—please DON'T blindly praise the course just because your grade is satisfying or you happen to like the subject. The truth is obvious to anyone paying attention. Of course, if ur an undercover agent planted by the instructor to write glowing reviews (just hypothetically speaking), then I’ve got nothing else to add. That’s beyond words.

This course is supposed to be foundational for full-stack development, yet it’s been handed to an instructor who is nothing short of a disaster. The lecture notes are a complete mess. Let’s start with the obvious errors. Especially in the material after the midterm, there were at least 5 glaring mistakes. These aren’t small ambiguities—they’re clear, misleading errors that should never have made it into teaching materials. Some of these mistakes are in example answers, and even key formulas are wrong. This is not an exaggeration; it’s all backed by evidence—just check the lecture note PDFs or the Canvas discussion boards.

What’s worse is that most of these mistakes were never fixed. The teaching team didn’t bother to correct them, even when students pointed them out in discussions. When they did respond, it was in a one-on-one reply buried in a thread, as if they were quietly whispering corrections in secret. So, let me ask: do you not care if the rest of the students remain misled? I never imagined that mistakes this serious wouldn’t deserve a public correction.

Here’s the most ridiculous example: one of the most misleading formula errors was corrected via a mass email ONE HOUR BEFORE THE FINAL EXAM. This error had already been flagged on the Canvas discussion board days earlier, but the TA’s response was a single reply to the student who raised it. Then—silence. Nothing. The issue disappeared into thin air until that last-minute email... I was outside the exam hall discussing the formula with a friend; he hadn’t seen the email and had memorised the wrong version.

And then there’s the utter chaos of the lecture notes themselves. They’re so badly organised that they’re practically unusable. A single lecture’s content is scattered across multiple pages, with titles that give no clue about the content. Is this some twisted attempt to confuse us? To artificially make the course seem harder with this jumbled mess? At one point, I even suspected the instructor had copy-pasted random notes from others without bothering to organise or check them. But even that doesn’t explain the SHEER NUMBER OF ERRORS.

In short, if you’re unlucky enough to take this course, do yourself a favour and ignore the lecture notes entirely. Even looking at them feels like an insult to the subject. Instead, search online forums for clear, logical explanations of the material. If you want proof of what I’m saying, just finish learning on other platforms and revisit the genius lecture notes. You’ll see the chaos, the errors, the confusion—and you’ll thank yourself for not relying on the lec notes to learn.
comment_teaching:
Honestly, writing this section feels like giving him more credit than he deserves—it’s a waste of my time and effort. The ONLY REASON I’m forcing myself to finish it is to make sure that anyone even slightly thinking about taking his course will give up on that idea completely.

Let’s start with how he gives lectures. He’s the perfect example of a “PPT reader.” Now, you might think there’s nothing special about PPT readers since you’ve probably seen plenty of them here. But no—please stick with me—because this man takes being a PPT reader to A WHOLE NEW LEVEL.

First, you’ll need at least 2–3 lectures just to figure out what he’s even saying. Ever heard someone talk in their sleep? That’s basically him. His tone is flat, his pronunciation is so unclear, and his voice never changes—it’s always at the same dull pitch. I suspect he’s afraid that changing his voice even slightly might accidentally wake up the students who’ve already fallen asleep.

Sometimes, his words don’t match the PPT exactly. That’s because his PPTs come with carefully written scripts. If you compare the scripts in his PPTs to what he says in the lecture recordings, you’ll see he reads them out word for word. One line that stayed with me was: “Any questions?”—yes, that was ACTUALLY WRITTEN in his script. Imagine how much effort it takes to write something like that! I have to give him credit—no detail is too small for this man. Oh, and here’s a little suggestion: next time, for a course like this, just hire any English-speaking person to read the slides aloud. It would be much cheaper than hiring a “distinguished professor” for the job. (Apparently, he eventually realised he forgot to delete the scripts from the PPTs, because later uploads didn’t have them anymore. )

Now let’s talk about his Paper Checking Masterclass. For a while, I thought his poor lecture style might be because of a limited grasp of English. But no—when I talked with him in Chinese, his thoughts were JUST AS MESSY. From his exam questions, I can tell he tried to play it safe by sticking to the style of past papers. Yet somehow, even with that, he still managed to write vague, confusing questions. The whole paper-checking process became a joke where students had to explain the questions back to the teaching team. Of course, the teaching team first insisted the questions were fine, with ziyi being the most stubborn of all. But in the end, they gave in and returned the points we deserved.

One particularly memorable case involved a clearly incorrect question on the final exam. Ziyi, however, stood his ground and refused to admit it. It got so bad that EVEN THE TA'S were too embarrassed to let it slide—one of them actually argued with ziyi on our behalf. Sure enough, several days later, many students got the points they were owed.

Oh, and let’s not forget his famous coupon stunt. I heard that in one lecture, he gave out a coupon to students who attended, allowing them to skip one question on the final exam (you can probably confirm this on USTSpace). Naturally, like most students, I didn’t attend that lecture, so I didn’t get the coupon. About this, I just have to say: absolutely amazing. Ziyi dared to add secret perks to the course without telling everyone, setting a new standard for secretly rewarding students. It’s his 1st year at UST, and I can’t imagine what kind of person thinks this is ok. Was this his way of punishing students who didn’t show up to his lectures? Was he trying to force us to attend despite his terrible teaching? Did he even think about the consequences of CHANGING THE SYLLABUS without clarifying it beforehand? Instead of trying to improve his teaching, he resorts to cheap tricks. I honestly can’t see how any responsible teacher would ever do this...

Some people say he’s new and deserves some patience, but let’s be clear—he taught in Australia for several years before coming here. This isn’t a rookie mistake; it’s a HABIT. I have no idea what’s going on in this man’s head: lectures that could put anyone to sleep, a refusal to admit mistakes when marking, and shady coupon distribution. This is ziyi—just the PLAIN FACTS. If you’re thinking about taking his course, the decision is yours.

Finally, let’s talk about the teaching team as a whole. Most of them can barely communicate properly in English. Their replies in the discussion forums are slow, unhelpful, and often miss the point. You can clearly feel the frustration and negativity in the questions students ask—it’s obvious in every word. But even with all of this, nothing compares to the disaster of ziyi’s teaching.
comment_workload: Minimal compared to average comp courses. If you don't care about grades or the learning experience, you might find the course acceptable. That said, the only homework assigned after the midterm covered just 1 TOPIC, while the final heavily tested THE REST. If you didn’t go through the supplementary materials on Canvas, well… good luck on the final.
comment_grading: Not bad, but be ready to rely ENTIRELY on self-learning. Much of the course content, especially before the midterm, is material many students may have already learned. To get a decent grade, you’ll need to put in extra effort and depend completely on yourself.

Without a speck of doubt in my mind, this is the worst course I have taken yet in ust - and this includes lang courses, comp1021 etc.

To make a course so bad is not easy task, and the instructor and TAs have done an excellent job in making this a contender for UST's worst comp course.

To start with, the TAs dont know jack shit about what is going in this course. There are 4 TAs in this course and I think each of them have a specific area they are supposed to deal with.

I mentioned there are 4 assignments in this course. In the first assignment, I went to the tutorial since I was confused when and when to not include "NOT NULL" statement since THERE IS NO FUCKING RULE STATED IN THE NOTES. AM I SUPPOSED TO GUESS IT MYSELF? Anyway, this is a key feature of this course - getting back to the topic. I go to the TA and ask this simple question, he said he does not know the answer and that I should contact another TA. I emailed said TA, and he never responded and voila, I get my marks deducted because I used "NOT NULL" where I should not have. Happened with not just me, but also others.

Exactly the same thing happened in assignment 2 and 3. Ditto same, I go to tut, I ask the TA and the TA said he does not know, and none of them respond to emails.

Besides not knowing jack shit, the guides for the labs (to install their old ass software) was wrong and took me 3 hours to install their software for a coding task worth 30 minutes maximum. Oh, one of the tutorials, the TA just didnt fucking show up.

Like this TAs, he said "I don't know, the TA made this question". WHO THE FUCK IS THIS ANONYMOUS TA I DON'T KNOW ABOUT AND CAN NOT CONTACT.

Anyways, that's it for the rant. Don't take this course.

comment_teaching:
Professors: LIU Ziyi

- Clear Projection of Voice
Ziyi is from mainland. I first thought his accent maybe unclear from my experience of attending lectures from mainland instructors. However, Ziyi has a very fluent speech and good projection of voice that I think he is different from other professor that I have met before. I can hear what he is saying easily even when not looking at the notes.

- Interesting teaching styles
Ziyi used many interesting examples to illustrate the abstract concepts in the lecture notes that is not mentioned in the notes. For example, he used the course registration system as an example to show how the ER diagram is drawn out instead of the plain definition of relationships and entity. I think this help me a lot in understanding the lecture materials and help me learn better.

- Quick response on questions
Ziyi always answer question raised by students after class. Even the students of next class arrived, he is willing to answer all of the question outside the lecture hall. Besides, he always respond to my email in a few hours. This shows he is really a enthusiastic and responsible teacher.

- Reinforcement in lecture
Since this is the first year for Ziyi to teach in HKUST. At first, I think he is not quite used to teach in a large room and face to a lot of student. He is first more focus on the teaching material part instead of the interaction between students. He finishes part of the material and ask student if we have any questions. Sometimes I cannot catch up well in certain part and not understand the part well. Later on, he includes more interaction with us so that we can catch up more easily and answer our questions more timely, which make my learning process faster. Besides, he tried to draw diagram on the whiteboard at front to show to all students at first. A student cannot see it clearly and run out to the front to take photo. After that, professor try to use the projector to display the drawing on paper, which I think he is good at reflecting his way of teaching and try to reinforce his lecture in certain way so that he can help student more in their learning. I appreciate that a lot.

- Bonus point
Ziyi has done a very interesting thing that I haven't seen other professor did throughout my entire UST life. Let me provide some background information first. This course does not require attendance in lecture, tutorial or lab session. So there are not many students attending the lecture. There are only around 20 students inside the big lecture theatre(LTA) which is for 400 students. As there is a serious attendance issue in the course. Professor gave a "COUPON" to each students who attended the lecture in one day. I was like wtf is a coupon when I heard him mentioning the giveaway. He gave each of us a coupon that can remove one question in the final exam. That means we can get some free mark in the final exam for free FOR FREE. He is the most generous and handsome professor in the department. No. in the whole campus. Just kidding. Desmond is better than him, hehe. But I really shocked when he give me the coupon as I have never seen such bonus of attending the lecture in a course that does not require attendance. I think he is really good, and I hope that he can give me more coupon that can be used in other courses.

lab only publishes at most 1 day before. hard to install so I recommend doing it in ust cse lab

Lecture topics:
Database Management Systems
-- Introduction to database, nothing special
Entity-Relationship (E-R) Model and Database Design
-- drawing ER diagram after reading comprehension of the question
Relational Algebra (RA)
-- another expression of SQL
Structured Query Language (SQL)
-- select from where ...
Relational Database Design
-- how to reduce redundancy but keeps functional dependency
*** Midterm test everything above ***
Storage and File Structure
-- how data is saved and retrieved(a little bit comp 2611)
Indexing
-- many different ways to reduce page I/O cost of retrieving data
Query Processing
-- highly related to indexing
Query Optimization
-- if you fail indexing and query processing, you cant understand anything here
Transactions
-- finally new easy things
Concurrency Control
-- how to run multiple programs at the same time while making sure everything reads and write in correct order
Recovery System
-- how to undo or redo after something crashes
*** Final test everything above ***
NoSQL Databases
-- not graded, yay

The content of this course is really huge, it is just like two course combining each other, you really need some time to understand it. Also, boring is definitely a problem when having too much information.

For the final, in past paper, you will see some question which is so called 玄學 (metaphysics by google translate) like calculating cost, join in QEP. However, for this professor, as what he said, his final exam does not require calculator. He doesn't require us to calculate join/select cost. He just ask for the principle and formula of the join which already teach us in tutorial and lecture. For query optimization, what you need is assumption base on the rule in notes, not imagination. His final exam is really better than other professor design since every question is reasonable.

But there are still some problems in the course. The answer of final and midterm exercise is not released. How do we know are there any mistake when the TA revising the examination papers? Also, the exam format is change before an hour of final. We suddenly require to finish the exam in word. However, a question require us to use some symbol and we don't know how to type it. This is so bad and I hope it won't happen again.

comment_content:
DO NOT TAKE THIS COURSE IF YOU'LL NOT WORK WITH DATABASES IN THE FUTURE! This course is very tough, very time-consuming and I DON'T recommend taking this for fun!! There are other fun things you can do with your life 🙂

(🔥 = Difficult/Confusing)
1️⃣ ER Model & Database Design
2️⃣ Relational Model
   🔹 Reduction of ER model
   🔥🔥 2NF, 3NF, BCNF
3️⃣ Relational Algebra
4️⃣ SQL
   🔹 Basic operations
   🔹 Aggregate functions
   🔹 DDL & DML
[📝 Mid-term]
5️⃣ Storage & File Structure
6️⃣ Indexing
   🔹 Single & multi-level indexing
   🔹 B+ tree index
   🔥 Hash index
7️⃣ Query Processing
   🔹 Selection cost & external sorting
   🔥 Join cost
   🔹 Projection, set operation cost
8️⃣🔥🔥 Query Optimization
9️⃣ Transactions & Concurrency Control
   🔹 Serializability
   🔹 Two-phased locking systems
   🔹 Timestamp protocols
🔟 Database Recovery
NoSQL Databases (not tested in final)
[📝 Final (cumulative)]

The first half of the course (before MT) is highly practical. It first teaches you the ER Model (similar to class diagrams in COMP3111) and how to extract information from a problem statement. Then, it teaches you the Relational Model, ie. converting the "class" diagram into different tables. Finally, there are 3 lessons on Oracle SQL where you learn how to make queries, create tables and insert table data.

However, the second half of the course is a LIVING HELL since everything is theoretical. You'll learn the data structures and algorithms used behind database management system and it's difficult as fuck 😪. The query optimization part is the most difficult and confusing since there're endless possibilities to evaluate a query and you need to provide the most optimized plan to process it. Most of the time I don't know what the fuck I'm doing 😩😩😩😩
comment_teaching:
TLDR: Professor is a nice guy with very fluent English. However, he basically SPEEDRUNS every lesson since there are too much content to cover and around 70% of the time you'll feel completely lost and confused at the end of the lecture 😫😫

📃 Teaching Style
He's fine in explaining the concepts on the slides and sometimes he emphasizes on some tricky parts and concepts. He is also very responsive to student's questions in Zoom (this semester is online due to COVID) and he can answer all the questions relatively quickly. However, every lesson is packed with rich content and he loves teaching at an INSANELY FAST speed, which is IMPOSSIBLE to catch up unless you're a genius. Every lesson feels like a world-record speedrun attempt and I usually get completely lost at around half way though the lesson 😪😪

The craziest lecture is lecture 5 on 2NF, 3NF and BCNF. Someone on YouTube has made a 6 hour playlist on the exact same content but the professor managed to cram all of that in a 1.5 hour lesson 😈. Just watch this playlist instead: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLdo5W4Nhv31b33kF46f9aFjoJPOkdlsRc

📃 Teaching Materials
The teaching materials are well designed in general. Each lecture has its own lecture slide, in-class worksheet and a worksheet solution (released at the next lesson), and they are well designed. However, the slides are a bit confusing to understand for the second half of the course.

📃 Tutorials & Labs
The "teaching" in tutorials is kinda shitty since the TA just reads out the tutorial slides and the exercise solutions. The labs exercises are pretty useful for you to practice writing SQL and PL/SQL though.
comment_workload:
TLDR: Feels like a 4-credits course. Shit ton of things to be submitted and requires lots of time to revise and study 😪😪😪.

📝Lesson, tutorial and lab exercises
This is fucking insane. We need to complete and submit a worksheet (1 or 2 pages) for every lesson, tutorial and lab (9 labs in total) at 11pm within the same date. These exercises can take from 5~10 minutes to as long as 1 hour to complete if you're answering them seriously. Since everyone get lost at the end of the lecture, you're forced to revise the lecture slides within the same day in order to know how to do those exercises and this is very time consuming.

📝Project
The project is also very time-consuming and each task takes me around 7~10 hours to complete. The first task is to draw an ER model based on a problem statement, which is quite difficult since you need to pay attention to many small details. The second task is even more annoying since you need to go through the problem statement multiple times in order to extract all the constraints that needed to be set when defining the tables in SQL. The third task is a little bit better, but we still need to write 40 short to medium SQL queries in a C# file.

📝Mid-term and Final
Since I got confused in almost every lesson, I need to spend a shit ton of time to revise the lecture slides when preparing for the mid-term and final exam. The final exam is also cumulative so I need to spend extra time revising for the first half of the course.

In summary, this feels like a 4-credits course. In fact, I've completed several 4-credits course like COMP2012, COMP3111 and MATH2411 and NONE of these courses have higher workload than this course
comment_grading:

I got full marks in lecture exercises, 26.5/30 marks in the project, +0.35 SD in mid-term and +0.88 SD in final. I got an A- at the end, which is totally out of my expectation. I feel like most of the students are confused with the course material so the mean is not unusually high 😛.

SQL

Storage and File Structure
Indexing

Although there are a few mistakes in the lecture notes, overall the lecture notes are well-designed. The content before midterm should be easier.

Most of the topics appeared in lectures will be in the exams. Tutorials just repeat the lecture content right after the lecture ends. Labs are not included in the exams. Attendance is not mandatory. The reference book is not mandatory.

You need to complete a worksheet after each lecture, tutorial and lab and submit on that day.

These worksheets are easy. They will be graded on efforts instead of correctness so full marks for sure. Very similar problems will also be demonstrated on class.

Skipping class is not a good idea. You don't get to watch the lecture videos shortly after the class. If you skip one, you probably can only rely on the lecture notes to complete the exercise before the day ends.
comment_teaching: Normal teaching. But sometimes the professor gets annoyed when there are quite many questions on zoom. He may just tell you to revise the previous ppt or give a unclear answer.
comment_workload:
8 worksheets per week but most of them are easy and you will have enough time to finish them on that day.

The three tasks in the project require much more time. You may need 2 days to complete it and spend so much time to debug and improve your work.

comment_content:
Covered about 80% chapters of the textbook - Database System Concepts, 7th edition. This could help you have a basic understanding of how to use DBMS (database management system) and how it internally works. Topics include:

- Database Design (E-R Model and Relational Model)
- Relational Language (Relational Algebra and SQL)
-------Midterm--------
- Storage Management and Indexing
- Query Processing and Optimization
- Transaction Control
-------Final------------
comment_teaching:
Prof. Lochovsky is nice and patient and he will reply to your email in a short time (probably on the same day). I guess he is a native English speaker so the teaching is clear and easy to understand. TA is nice and willing to answer your questions too.

The teaching speed before midterm is moderate. But after midterm it may be a bit fast as there are so many knowledges to teach in each lecture. You probably cannot totally understand it just during the lectures and it is advisable to pre-view or review notes after midterm.
comment_workload:
A bit great for summer. I need to spend the whole class day - in the morning and afternoon attending class, in the evening doing lecture exercises, tutorials and labs. The DDL of them is 11pm on the same day of class so you cannot do it another day. Fortunately, you just have 2 class day in a week.

Each of the 3 project task requires additional 1-2 day if you want to get high marks. But you can easily finish it in half day to get average marks.
comment_grading:
Not sure whether it is good or bad grade. Some stat for reference:

- Midterm (20%): Mean 75.2/100 SD ~12 (I forgot) High 97
- Final (40%): Mean 66.17/100 SD 14.3 High 89.75
- Project (3 Task, each task 10%): Mean for each task is 7~8
- Lecture Exercise, Tutorial & Lab (10%): Almost everybody get nearly full mark as long as you submit it

I got 1 SD above mean in midterm and 1.5 SD above mean in final, project a bit above mean. Finally got A+.

Exam is open-book but understanding is very important. It is not necessary to understand all the materials in notes just for exam as some of them will not be tested. However, I think you should understand all the exercises (including lecture exercises and tutorials) if you want to get good grade.

comment_content: Before midterm, the course is about relation and SQL. After midterm, it's about query processing and query optimization.
comment_teaching: Prof is very responsible and he always replies email almost immediately. The feedback of exercises, tasks, and exams are very timely. The PPT, exercise, and exercise solution have some minor problems. Though they do not affect the understanding of the basic concepts, it causes some trouble. And if you do not point them out and double-check with prof, you might never know there're some problems with the materials, which is misleading. In order to be on schedule, prof sometimes just briefly explain some materials. I still need to digest the content a lot after class.
comment_workload: The workload is huge for a summer course. We have lectures on Tuesday and Thursday. On the days having lectures, we need to hand-in two in-class exercises, one tutorial exercise, and one lab exercise. The content is so much that it is quite hard to catch up. Besides the exercises, we need to finish three projects, which are based on lab materials. The final is cumulative, so the review is also not easy.

comment_content: The content is too much for a course, before the midterm is about SQL and some basic logical knowledge on database, after the midterm is all about the physical database, indexing techniques, some other programming languages. If you can learn everything well, then you can have a really good understanding on DB system. But there's too much for a single lecture and it's really hard to get familiar with everything taught. So be careful to plan your workload when you enroll in this course.
comment_teaching: Wilfred has basically well-prepared PowerPoint, as for the teaching, it's completely just reading the PowerPoint, I don't know if it's because Zoom teaching but it's possibly the same in the classrooms, skipping all the lectures is not recommended though, it's better to attend the lectures even you just read the PPT through without noticing what he's saying.
comment_workload:
The workload is not that tough, there are four assignments, basically just some implementation on the lecture notes, so if you learn them carefully you'll be able to manage the assignment really well.
As for the exams, the problems types are almost fixed and you really need to do more practice on the pastpapers, they will have you get a good understanding on the exams.
comment_grading: The grading is not good, I think getting a good grade is pretty hard. You need to do really well on the exams and try to get full on the homeworks.

comment_content: Only 3 lessons on SQL. I have learnt it so I do pretty well on mid-term. The content before midterm was make sense. However, the content after midterm was 3 or 4 times before the midterm. Most of the content you would not use again, except you want to be a database manager in the future. This content was not just about SQL, it covered most of the materials in cs, like comp2611, comp3511, c++, networking, web, java. You take one course, you can gain 10 courses' knowledge. I think the huge different style before and after midterm was because the textbook. This guy chose two different  textbooks and the styles of them were totally different. He just copied and pasted the content from the textbook to his ppt so it made the style quite different after midterm. Most of the topic on final, like PL/SQL and database API and web API, he even did not teach them or just skip them. However, the questions were very difficult in the final exam.
comment_teaching: This guy was even worse than Dekai. This is not a joke. He just read the ppt every lesson and rushed very fast after midterm since the content was like a 5-credits course. Sometime, I asked him some questions, but he even could not answer me correctly. If you do not trust me how bad he was, just do it!!!! just do it!!!!
comment_workload: Workload is very light. The good thing is you don't need to do too much but the bad thing is obvious. You would not have chance to do practice as the assignment could only cover around 5% of the materials after the mid-term (only 1 pa after midterm but the content was 3 times before). He would post some self-practice exercise on his website, I finished them all and the exercise in tutor but I think this was still not enough.
comment_grading:
Since this semester conducted online exam, so the SQL part would directly run by machine, no any marks for your steps. You would got 0 if your SQL cannot compile on his machine. Thus, this was quite unfair because everyone might have typo in his/her code. I got full mark in the 4 assignments and attendance, 9x/100 in midterm and 7x/100 in final (the final was extremely difficult).
The above is what I have typed before releasing the grade and I want to give it another E on grading orginally (If you have seen other reviews in other semesters, you would know how bad the grade was). But honestly, I got A+ finally. Then, I think giving it a B on grading is quite fair. However, you cannot take this as a consideration. I think he gave all of us very good grade on this semester because of online teaching. I believe I can only get A- at most at the regular term. You should take some reviews in other semesters as a consideration.
